target.name: (+)-Talaromyolide D
target.motivation: Talaromyolide D (4) was uniquely active among the family members and displayed antiviral activity against pseudorabies virus (PRV). It features a rare dimethylcyclobutanol subunit as an equatorial substituent at the C18 position of the D ring, which may contribute to its distinct bioactivity. This substructure is part of a series of four contiguous stereocenters, two of which are quaternary and feature 1,3-diaxial methyl groups locked in a contrathermodynamic orientation. The distinctive skeletal features and stereochemical complexity make it an attractive target for chemical synthesis, offering significant value for structural confirmation and further biological evaluation.

success_criteria.free_text: Comparison of spectral data (especially 13C NMR and NOE correlations) to the reported natural product sample.

key_disconnections.reasoning: Maximizes convergence of the route and reveals bifunctional fragments 10 and 11, which could be sequentially stitched together via a beta-C(sp3)-H arylation and an intramolecular SNAr cyclization strategy.

This critical bond joining the E and D ring system could be formed via a nickel-catalyzed electrochemical decarboxylative sp2-sp3 cross-coupling between the redox-active ester located on the cyclobutane ring of 13 and beta-iodoenone 14.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C6-H and C2-H bonds of the A-ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: A carboxylic acid residue embedded within the A-ring of the lactone could serve as a directing handle for two separate C-H activation steps. This allows differentiation between the two positions to first perform an ortho-C-H hydroxylation at the more accessible C6 position, followed by a second ortho-C-H lactonization reaction at the C2 position.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy is convergent and stereoselective, enabled by a stereoretentive, nickel-catalyzed electrochemical sp2-sp3 decarboxylative cross-coupling, a two-fold bidirectional stitching sequence comprised of an oxime-directed beta-C(sp3)-H arylation and SNAr to establish the central ring system, and a series of two late-stage carboxylic-acid-directed C(sp2)-H oxidation reactions to ultimately access the isocoumarin lactone substructure. This approach leverages C-H activation logic to streamline access to the molecule.

thought.background: The synthesis began with the preparation of the cis-substituted cyclobutane fragment 16 from (-)-verbenone (15).
thought.gap: Need to convert the alkene and then the resulting carboxylic acid to a redox-active N-hydroxyphthalimide (NHPI) ester.
thought.decision: To prepare fragment 16, chose oxidative cleavage of the alkene, followed by Baeyer-Villiger oxidation and coupling with N-hydroxyphthalimide.

outcome.observation: Fragment 16 was prepared in three steps on a decagram scale from (-)-verbenone (15).

evidence.text_span: The synthesis of talaromyolide D (4) began with the preparation of the cis-substituted cyclobutane fragment 16. This fragment was prepared in three steps on a decagram scale from $( - )$ -verbenone (15) (Scheme 1). Oxidative cleavage7 of the alkene residue (ruthenium trichloride, sodium periodate) and subsequent treatment of the unpurified reaction mixture with meta-chloroperoxybenzoic acid ( $m$ -CPBA) provided the expected C27 Baeyer−Villiger oxidation product (not shown). The resulting C24 carboxylic residue was then converted to the redox-active N-hydroxyphthalimide (NHPI) ester 16 by coupling with $N$ -hydroxyphthalimide mediated by 1-ethyl-3- (3-(dimethylamino)propyl)carbodiimide (EDC; $7 1 \%$ from 15).

thought.background: Fragment 16 was prepared in ample quantities.
thought.gap: Need to perform the key cross-coupling between 16 and vinyl iodide 17 to form the E-D bicycle 20.
thought.decision: To achieve the cross-coupling, chose Baran’s nickel catalyzed decarboxylative electrochemical cross-coupling enabled by silver nanoparticle-modified electrodes.

outcome.observation: The desired E-D bicycle 20 was obtained in 85% yield and in 11:1 dr at the C24 position. The stereochemistry was established by conversion to crystalline alcohol 21.

evidence.text_span: With ample quantities of fragment 16, we next investigated the key cross-coupling between 16 and vinyl iodide 17. To this end, we found that Baran’s nickel catalyzed decarboxylative electrochemical cross-coupling enabled by silver nanoparticle-modified electrodes provided the desired E−D bicycle 20 in $8 5 \%$ yield and in 11:1 dr at the C24 position.8 The stereochemistry of the cross-coupled product was established by conversion of 20 to the crystalline alcohol 21 (potassium hydroxide, methanol, $9 7 \%$ ).

thought.background: The nickel-mediated radical cross-coupling reaction delivered the desired coupled product with excellent stereocontrol (11:1 dr).
thought.gap: Need to understand the origin of the high stereocontrol, especially given the absence of chiral ligands.
thought.decision: Hypothesized that the high level of stereocontrol reflects the favorable conformation of the cyclobutane radical (18), which minimizes nonbonding interactions and maximizes overlap between the radical SOMO and the adjacent C-C bond. This implies that free radical racemization should outcompete nickel-mediated radical capture in the absence of substrate-conferred stability.

outcome.observation: Hypothesis formed regarding the stereocontrol mechanism.

evidence.text_span: This $\mathsf { s p } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { - } \mathsf { s p } ^ { 3 }$ radical cross-coupling reaction takes place in the absence of any chiral ligands at the nickel center and delivers the desired coupled product with excellent stereocontrol. We hypothesize that the high level of stereocontrol observed during the coupling reflects the favorable conformation of the cyclobutane radical, as depicted in structure 18. This conformation minimizes nonbonding interactions between the acetate and the C25 methyl group while maximizing overlap between the radical SOMO and the adjacent $\mathrm { C - C }$ bond.9 Recent work by Diao and co-workers demonstrated that the binding of a secondary radical to a nickel(II)−aryl species is a relatively slow process $\left( k = 1 0 ^ { 6 } \mathrm { ~ M ~ } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { ~ s ~ } ^ { - 1 } ight)$ and is also sensitive to the steric environment surrounding the nickel catalyst.10 This implies that free radical racemization (picosecond time scale) should outcompete nickel-mediated radical capture in the absence of any substrate-conferred stability.11

thought.background: Hypothesis formed regarding the stereocontrol of the cross-coupling.
thought.gap: Need to verify the hypothesis that the secondary radical preferentially reacts via the more accessible convex face of the Ni(II) species.
thought.decision: To verify the hypothesis, prepared redox-active ester 16 and its C24-epimer as a 1:1.4 mixture and exposed it to the standard reaction conditions.

outcome.observation: The E-D bicycle 20 was obtained in 83% yield with an identical 11:1 dr at C24, supporting the hypothesis that the secondary radical 18 preferentially reacts via the more accessible convex face of the Ni(II) species 19.

evidence.text_span: To verify our hypothesis, we prepared redox-active ester 16 and its C24- epimer as a 1:1.4 mixture via an alternative ring contraction strategy (see Supporting Information). Exposure of this mixture of diastereomers to the standard reaction conditions provided the $\mathrm { E - D }$ bicycle 20 in $8 3 \%$ yield with an identical 11:1 dr at C24 (Scheme 2A). This result indicates that the secondary radical 18 preferentially reacts via the more accessible convex face of the $\operatorname { N i } ( \operatorname { I I } )$ species 19, through either a stereoretentive stepwise or concerted inner-sphere mechanism to deliver the desired enone 20.

thought.background: The E-D bicycle 20 was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: Need to elaborate bicycle 20 to introduce the C18 methyl group and form the exocyclic alpha,beta-unsaturated ketone 23.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose a three-component protocol: diastereoselective nickel-catalyzed conjugate addition of trimethylaluminum to the C18 position, in situ trapping of the resulting aluminum enolate with N-(methoxymethyl)-diethylamine, followed by oxidative elimination of the tertiary diethyl amine group.

outcome.observation: Exocyclic alpha,beta-unsaturated ketone 23 was obtained in 73% yield as a 3:1 ratio of diastereomers at the C18 position. The configuration of the newly formed stereocenter was established by X-ray analysis of benzoate 25.

evidence.text_span: The bicycle 20 was elaborated by a three-component protocol starting first with a diastereoselective nickel-catalyzed conjugate addition of trimethylaluminum to the sterically hindered C18 position of the $\alpha { , }  eta$ -unsaturated ketone $2 0 $ 22).13 In situ trapping of the resulting aluminum enolate 22 with N-(methoxymethyl)-diethylamine,14 followed by oxida- tive elimination of tertiary diethyl amine group (m-CPBA) provided the exocyclic $\alpha { , }  eta$ -unsaturated ketone 23 in $7 3 \%$ yield as a 3:1 ratio of diastereomers at the C18 position $\mathrm { ^ { 1 } H }$ NMR analysis). The configuration of the newly formed stereocenter was established by X-ray analysis of benzoate 25 (vide infra).

thought.background: Exocyclic alpha,beta-unsaturated ketone 23 was obtained as a 3:1 mixture of diastereomers.
thought.gap: Need to improve the stereoselectivity of the conjugate addition.
thought.decision: Attempted to improve stereoselectivity by using copper salts and chiral phosphoramidite ligands.

outcome.observation: Efforts to improve the stereoselectivity by using copper salts and chiral phosphoramidite ligands were ineffective.

evidence.text_span: Efforts to improve the stereoselectivity by using copper salts and chiral phosphoramidite ligands were ineffective (see the Supporting Information).

thought.background: Attempts to improve stereoselectivity of the conjugate addition were ineffective, resulting in a diastereomeric mixture of 23.
thought.gap: Need to obtain a single diastereomer for further synthesis.
thought.decision: Separated the diastereomeric mixture by flash-column chromatography on preparative scales.

outcome.observation: The diastereomeric mixture was separated by flash-column chromatography on preparative scales.

evidence.text_span: Fortunately, the diastereomeric mixture was separated by flash-column chromatography on preparative scales.

thought.background: The exocyclic alpha,beta-unsaturated ketone 23 was obtained and separated.
thought.gap: Need to reduce the alkene residue and form the cis-dimethyl ketone 24 with high diastereoselectivity at the C15 position.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose reduction of the alkene residue with palladium on carbon and dihydrogen, followed by exposure of the reaction mixture to hydrogen chloride at elevated temperatures.

outcome.observation: The cis-dimethyl ketone 24 was obtained with 5:1 diastereoselectivity at the C15 position in 75% yield. Acidic treatment was critical for high diastereoselectivity. The stereochemistry of 24 was confirmed by acylation of the C27 alcohol residue with para-nitrobenzoyl chloride, which provided the crystalline 25.

evidence.text_span: Reduction of the alkene residue (palladium on carbon, dihydrogen) and exposure of the reaction mixture to hydrogen chloride at elevated temperatures $( 1 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } )$ provided the cis-dimethyl ketone 24 with 5:1 diastereoselectivity at the C15 position in $7 5 \%$ yield ( $^ 1 \mathrm { H }$ NMR analysis). Notably, the acidic treatment was critical to achieve high levels of diastereoselectivity, as upon enolization of the $\alpha$ -proton the reprotonation occurs so that the C15 methyl group is oriented anti to the steric bulk of the cyclobutane moiety. The stereochemistry of 24 was confirmed by acylation of the C27 alcohol residue with para-nitrobenzoyl chloride, which provided the crystalline 25 with the desired cis-dimethyl groups.

thought.background: The cis-dimethyl ketone 24 was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: Need to prepare the oxime ether 26 for the subsequent C-H arylation.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose condensation of O-methylhydroxylamine with the C15 carbonyl and protection of the C27 alcohol with methoxymethyl chloride and Hünig’s base.

outcome.observation: Oxime ether 26 was delivered in 66% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: To this end, condensation of O-methylhydroxylamine with the C15 carbonyl and protection of the C27 alcohol (methoxymethyl chloride, Hünig’s base) delivered the oxime ether 26 in $6 6 \%$ yield over two steps.

thought.background: Oxime ether 26 was successfully prepared.
thought.gap: Need to introduce the aromatic group via C(sp3)-H arylation of the C15 methyl group, directed by the proximal oxime ether group.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose exposure of 26 to diaryliodonium salt 28 in the presence of palladium acetate, silver carbonate, and substoichiometric amounts of pivalic acid.

outcome.observation: The beta-arylated product 27 was smoothly delivered in 82% yield.

evidence.text_span: The aromatic group was then introduced to our substrate via efficient $\mathrm { C } ( \mathrm { s p } ^ { 3 } ) \mathrm { - } \hat { \mathrm { H } }$ arylation of the C15 methyl group directed by the proximal oxime ether group. Specifically, exposure of 26 to diaryliodonium salt 28 in the presence of palladium acetate, silver carbonate, and substoichiometric amounts of pivalic acid smoothly delivered the $ eta$ -arylated product 27 in $8 2 \%$ yield.

thought.background: The beta-arylated product 27 was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to remove the oxime ether directing group.
thought.decision: Attempted to remove the oxime ether directing group under acidic conditions.

outcome.observation: Removing the oxime ether directing group under acidic conditions resulted in unwanted epimerization of the stereochemistry at the C15 position.

evidence.text_span: We next attempted to remove the oxime ether directing group under acidic conditions, but this resulted in the unwanted epimerization of the stereochemistry at the C15 position.

thought.background: Acidic conditions failed to remove the oxime ether group without epimerization.
thought.gap: Need an alternative method for selective oxidative cleavage of the oxime ether.
thought.decision: Attempted selective oxidative cleavage of the oxime ether with photoexcited nitroarenes or with ozone.

outcome.observation: Efforts to perform selective oxidative cleavage of the oxime ether with photoexcited nitroarenes or with ozone were inefficient.

evidence.text_span: Similarly, efforts to perform selective oxidative cleavage of the oxime ether with photoexcited nitroarenes or with ozone were inefficient (see the Supporting Information).16

thought.background: Previous attempts to remove the oxime ether directing group failed due to epimerization or inefficiency.
thought.gap: Need a method to remove the oxime ether and hydrolyze to the desired ketone without epimerization, followed by Wittig olefination.
thought.decision: To overcome this, employed low-valent titanium (titanium isopropoxide, ethylmagnesium bromide) to reductively cleave the N-O bond of the oxime ether and furnish the corresponding imine, which could be hydrolyzed to the desired ketone by the addition of boric acid. Subsequent Wittig olefination was then performed.

outcome.observation: The expected alkene product 29 was delivered in 52% yield over three steps.

evidence.text_span: To overcome this, we employed low-valent titanium (titanium isopropoxide, ethylmagnesium bromide) to reductively cleave the N−O bond of the oxime ether and furnish the corresponding imine, which could be hydrolyzed to the desired ketone by the addition of boric acid.17 Subsequent Wittig olefination (potassium tert-butoxide, methyl triphenylphosphonium bromide) delivered the expected alkene product 29 $( 5 2 \%$ , three steps).

thought.background: Alkene product 29 was successfully obtained.
thought.gap: Need to construct the C-ring of the target and provide the tetracycle 30.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose a two-step sequence comprising a diastereoselective iron-catalyzed Mukaiyama-type alkene hydration and intramolecular SNAr of the generated tertiary alcohol with the pendant aryl-fluoride.

outcome.observation: Tetracycle 30 was provided in 80% yield over two steps. The Mukaiyama hydration provided the desired C16 tertiary alcohol as a single detectable diastereomer, and the diastereoselectivity was determined by strong NOE correlations between the C16 and C18 methyl groups in the annulated product 31.

evidence.text_span: Next, a two-step sequence comprising a diastereoselective iron-catalyzed Mukaiyama-type alkene hydration18 and intramolecular $\mathrm { S } _ { \mathrm { N } } \mathrm { Ar }$ (sodium hydride) of the generated tertiary alcohol with the pendant aryl-fluoride constructed the C-ring of the target and provided the tetracycle 30 in $8 0 \%$ yield over two steps. The Mukaiyama hydration provided the desired C16 tertiary alcohol as a single detectable diastereomer $^ { 1 } \mathrm { H }$ NMR analysis) and the diastereoselectivity was determined by strong NOE correlations between the C16 and C18 methyl groups in the annulated product 31.

thought.background: Tetracycle 30 was successfully obtained.
thought.gap: Need to hydrolyze the iso-propyl ester to reveal the corresponding carboxylic acid residue, which can direct C-H activation.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose hydrolysis of the iso-propyl ester.

outcome.observation: The iso-propyl ester was hydrolyzed to the corresponding carboxylic acid residue (31).

evidence.text_span: To this end, we first hydrolyzed the iso-propyl ester to reveal the corresponding carboxylic acid residue, which we envisioned could direct $\mathrm { { \dot { C } } { - } H }$ activation preferentially at the less hindered C6 position, followed by a second $\mathrm { C - H }$ activation reaction at the more sterically hindered ortho-C2 site to systematically functionalize the B-ring.

thought.background: Carboxylic acid 31 was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to perform a palladium catalyzed C-H hydroxylation at the C6 position with good selectivity.
thought.decision: Attempted C-H hydroxylation using hydrogen peroxide as the oxygen source in combination with Yu’s CarboxPyridone ligand L1.

outcome.observation: The conditions successfully oxidized the aromatic ring, but the selectivity between the two ortho-positions was only modest (1.4:1 C6:C2), favoring the desired C6 oxidation.

evidence.text_span: Our initial attempts to affect a palladium catalyzed $\mathrm { C - H }$ hydroxylation at the C6 position focused on employing hydrogen peroxide as the “oxygen” source in combination with Yu’s CarboxPyridone ligand L1 (Table 1, Entry 1 and 2).19 While these conditions were able to successfully oxidize our aromatic ring, the selectivity between the two ortho-positions was only modest, favoring the desired C6 oxidation.

thought.background: C-H hydroxylation with L1 and H2O2 showed modest regioselectivity.
thought.gap: Need to improve regioselectivity for C6 oxidation.
thought.decision: Attempted C-H hydroxylation with L1 and H2O2 at elevated temperature with CsOAc.

outcome.observation: The selectivity improved slightly to 2.3:1 C6:C2, but was still not sufficient.

evidence.text_span: Our initial attempts to affect a palladium catalyzed $\mathrm { C - H }$ hydroxylation at the C6 position focused on employing hydrogen peroxide as the “oxygen” source in combination with Yu’s CarboxPyridone ligand L1 (Table 1, Entry 1 and 2).19 While these conditions were able to successfully oxidize our aromatic ring, the selectivity between the two ortho-positions was only modest, favoring the desired C6 oxidation.

thought.background: Previous attempts at C-H hydroxylation showed modest regioselectivity.
thought.gap: Need to achieve high regioselectivity for C6 oxidation.
thought.decision: To achieve high regioselectivity, employed molecular oxygen along with the tautomeric pyridine-pyridone ligand L2 and 1,4-benzoquinone at elevated reaction temperature, followed by treatment with diazomethane.

outcome.observation: The desired C6 hydroxylated methyl ester 33 was furnished as the C6 exclusive regioisomer in 73% yield.

evidence.text_span: However, employing molecular oxygen along with the tautomeric pyridine−pyridone ligand L2 and 1,4-benzoquinone at elevated reaction temperate furnished the desired C6 hydroxylated methyl ester 33 after treatment with diazomethane as the C6 exclusive regioisomer in $7 3 \%$ yield.20

thought.background: C6 hydroxylated methyl ester 33 was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to protect the phenol residue and hydrolyze the ester to obtain acid 34.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose protection of the phenol residue with sodium hydride and benzyl bromide, followed by hydrolysis of the ester.

outcome.observation: Acid 34 was obtained in 76% over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Elaboration of 33 via protection of the phenol residue (sodium hydride, benzyl bromide) and hydrolysis of the ester gave acid 34 $7 6 \%$ over two steps).

thought.background: Acid 34 was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to construct the final lactone ring of 4 via a C-H alkylation-lactonization sequence at the C2 position.
thought.decision: Attempted a palladium-catalyzed alkylation at the C2 position with epoxide 35.

outcome.observation: The reaction only returned unreacted starting material or decomposition of 34 at elevated reaction temperatures.

evidence.text_span: We then envisioned constructing the final lactone ring of 4 via a C−H alkylation− lactonization sequence (Scheme 2B).21 However, our attempts to perform a palladium-catalyzed alkylation at the C2 position with epoxide 35 were unsuccessful. In all cases, the reaction only returned unreacted starting material or we observed decomposition of 34 at elevated reaction temperatures.

thought.background: Palladium-catalyzed C-H alkylation failed.
thought.gap: Need an alternative strategy to introduce the desired carbon-carbon bond at the C2 position and form the lactone.
thought.decision: Explored a strategy based on directed lithiation.

outcome.observation: This approach delivered only limited quantities of the desired lactone 38 (8% yield).

evidence.text_span: A strategy based on directed lithiation was also explored but this approach delivered only limited quantities of the desired lactone 38 (Scheme 2B).22

thought.background: Previous attempts to introduce the C2 carbon-carbon bond and form the lactone failed or gave low yields.
thought.gap: Need a robust method to introduce the C2 carbon-carbon bond and form the lactone.
thought.decision: To address this difficult challenge, pursued a cross-coupling-based approach. First, performed a second C-H hydroxylation to arrive at the C2 hydroxylated species 39.

outcome.observation: The C2 hydroxylated species 39 was obtained in 85% yield.

evidence.text_span: To address this difficult challenge of introducing the desired carbon−carbon bond at the C2 position, we pursued a cross-coupling-based approach (Scheme 1). First, we performed a second C−H hydroxylation to arrive at the C2 hydroxylated species 39 $( 8 5 \% )$ .19

thought.background: C2 hydroxylated species 39 was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to convert the hydroxyl group to a triflate and then perform a Stille cross-coupling to introduce the allyl group at C2.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose triflation with sodium hydride and phenyl triflimide, followed by a Stille cross-coupling with allyltributylstannane.

outcome.observation: The C2 allyl product 40 was provided in 86% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: This was followed by a triflation (sodium hydride and phenyl triflimide) and a Stille cross-coupling with allyltributylstannane to provide the C2 allyl product 40 in $8 6 \%$ yield over two steps.

thought.background: C2 allyl product 40 was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to convert the allyl group to a methyl ketone at C9 and then reduce it to the requisite secondary alcohol with high diastereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose Wacker-Tsuji oxidation to deliver a C9 methyl ketone, followed by reduction with (S)-2-butyl-CBS-oxazaborolidine and borane dimethyl sulfide.

outcome.observation: Wacker-Tsuji oxidation delivered a C9 methyl ketone (not shown), which was reduced to the requisite secondary alcohol with 2:1 diastereoselectivity. The unpurified reaction mixture was directly treated with hydrogen chloride to complete the formation of the lactone ring and simultaneously remove the C27 methoxymethyl ether, providing the lactone 38 in 75% over three steps.

evidence.text_span: Wacker−Tsuji oxidation (palladium dichloride, copper(I) chloride, oxygen) delivered a C9 methyl ketone (not shown), which was reduced to the requisite secondary alcohol with 2:1 diastereoselectivity ( $^ \mathrm { { \Delta ^ { - 1 } H } }$ NMR analysis) utilizing a Corey−Bakshi−Shibata reduction ((S)-2- butyl-CBS-oxazaborolidine, borane dimethyl sulfide, 2:1 dr at C9).23 Notably, while performing this reduction, we first identified that bulky hydride-reducing agents $\left( \mathrm { L i A l H } ( \mathbf { O } t \mathbf { - B u } ) _ { 3 } ight.$ or L−selectride) favored the desired secondary alcohol diastereomer (Scheme 1, Table 2). In our attempts to improve the diastereoselectivity, we sought to explore a reagent-controlled reductions with CBS catalysts. As seen in Table 2, we found that the larger 2-butyl containing oxazaborolidine provided improved selectivity. As the secondary alcohol product was found to undergo a partial translactonization, the unpurified reaction mixture was directly treated with hydrogen chloride to complete the formation of the lactone ring and simultaneously remove the C27 methoxymethyl ether, providing the lactone 38 $7 5 \%$ over three steps).

thought.background: Lactone 38 was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to cleave the benzyl protecting group to obtain synthetic talaromyolide D (4).
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose cleavage of the benzyl protecting group with palladium on carbon and dihydrogen.

outcome.observation: Synthetic talaromyolide D (4) was delivered in 99% yield. X-ray crystallographic analysis of 4 served to confirm its structure as well as all of the established stereoselectivity.

evidence.text_span: Finally, cleavage of the benzyl protecting group (palladium on carbon and dihydrogen) delivered synthetic talaromyolide D (4) in $9 9 \%$ yield. X-ray crystallographic analysis of 4 served to confirm its structure as well as all of the established stereoselectivity.

validation.comparison_to_prior: NMR spectroscopic data of synthetic 4 were not in agreement with the reported spectrum for the isolated sample. Close inspection of the 13C NMR spectra identified a total of five carbons with large discrepancies (absolute error (AE) = >4 ppm), located around or as part of the cyclobutane E-ring. Additionally, the synthetic sample displayed strong NOE correlations between the two diaxial methyl substituents at C16 and C18 that are noticeably absent in the reported ROESY experiment performed on the isolated sample.
validation.significance: The synthesis provides the first access to the reported structure of talaromyolide D (4). The discrepancy in spectral data suggests that the structure of the natural product had been misassigned, and the synthetic strategy may present an opportunity for further structural elucidation of the talaromyolide family isolates. Computational analysis suggests that multiple stereocenters may be misassigned and that further revisions to the proposed bond connectivity may be necessary.

source.title: Total Synthesis of the Nominal Structure of (+)-Talaromyolide D.
source.authors: Qin B, Szyperek A, Tomanik M.
